sentences.text: Yes, five weeks.
The Jews started paying money.
The mayor of the town, which came from Romania, was a lawyer, Traian Popovici, a local native, you could call it, so which returned.
And he was named-- he named as mayor of the town.
As a governor, was a Professor Alexianu which was very known for his anti-Semitism.
He was a professor of law in Czernowitz University before.
And then afterwards was a general.
I was under him only a few days, because Professor Alexianu was nominated as governor of Transnistria.
He was afterwards in charge of the deportees.
Uh, professor-- Dr.
Popovici started giving out permits for Jews.
In order to get the permit, you had to be a Jew necessary for the economy of the town, of the country, of the German war effort, or you paid.
And who had paid?
The-- not all, but you were deported in the same, in the first weeks of November, 30,000 Jews.
We-- our family and aunt, we went to the Umschlagplatz.
Just before you-- you go to that story, was-- were you aware of any kind of Judenrat set up in the Cernauti ghetto, in that short time.
We had our a community, which was under the Russians, they continued dealing.
See and they were the official which the-- spokesmen of the Jewish population.
Some were former colleagues of Dr.
Popovici, this lawyer, which were very pro-Jewish in general.
And they paid.
To save as many Jews as possible.
No, money.
The Romanian-- the Romanian took money.
It was corruption as I told, all this was--
Not.
And I will you later in June 1942, the Jews who had permit were sent off.
Time, yes.
The first in November.
We were sent from the train.
I want to remember.
If you remember, I mentioned my colleagues which were-- had subscription to my homework.
One of them was a officer in the Romanian army.
And he was one of the officer who had the-- in charge of our embarkation, if I could call it so, [NON-ENGLISH].
He was officer already, not a great friend to Jews.
He was wounded in Odessa.
So he was on this job.
He was a known [INAUDIBLE] man.
But when he saw me, he, oh, he recognize me.
He say, can I help you with something?
And I said, I would like to see my aunt, the younger sister of my late mother.
She was with the other group, with his family, [NON-ENGLISH] family, our father and stepmother, and about 30 people.
So they're together in one carriage.
He went with me to them, and I spoke.
I tried to ask my aunt to come over to our carriage, but he didn't want to separate from his sister, and brother-in-law, and so on.
So it was the last time I saw them alive.
And, uh, I returned.
He put me two bottles of water in, told the officer-- the soldiers-- he didn't go with us-- but he told the soldiers that-- to help me, if possible, not to shoot me or something.
And, uh, we were closed in.
Yes, he took four or five Jews out from my carriage to give us more [NON-ENGLISH].
He put them in the other carriage, which had already-- I think these were 50 in a carriage, 50 or 60 in a carriage.
And this thing started moving.
We were sent first to-- went over [?
LIpcani, ?] or [NON-ENGLISH], toward Dniester.
We stopped in-- once we stopped somewhere in a field, there are some village.
And to us this Romanian were closed, but they opened the door.
And because of stench and all people some were vomiting, some were going mad.
I remember two women who went mad, berserk.
And they started [INAUDIBLE].
And they started running, pushing themselves with the-- with the [INAUDIBLE], pushing on this.
And there's knives on this.
And my father had the hernia.
This came out.
And I had to push it back.
It was not very pleasant.
But water was important.
I had to bottle it to let go through who needed it.
Children were hurt, old people-- and I ask the soldier permission to go water.
He knew me, because his officer told him.
So he let me go about 50 yards to bring some water.
It was not the cleanest water, but it was water.
I brought it back.
They didn't shoot, they let me go.
And from [NON-ENGLISH], the head for typhoid, they say, uh, department there.
It's not typhoid, [INAUDIBLE].
They had some doctors there, [INAUDIBLE].
They let also one of the doctors go.
And my cousin, which was with us, he had a friend who was doctor so he was his nurse, male nurse.
He was also permitted to go for water.
So it was in the whole train, about 5, 6.
It was a train about 30 carriages.
It was about thousand, 600, or 800 people.
So you can imagine, it was not much water left.
After two days, we were at Otaci That is on the-- a town on the Dniester.
We were stopped outside the town-- usually in Bessarabia is a [NON-ENGLISH].
It was outside the town.
The railway station was outside the town.
And to [INAUDIBLE] there, we were taken over by Romanian soldiers, gendarmerie, uh, local militia, Ukrainian.
They appeared-- they had arm bands, yellow arm bands, And of course, German.
German were all the time filming.
All the times we were standing there a few points where they were filming.
And of course, the officer-- forwards, forwards, forwards to go.
But mostly, the Romanians were in charge.
We went into a stop near a synagogue, old synagogue, which was burned down, and there had to deposit all the document.
And it was-- it's a public law, at the same time, because you went there in, no one to check you.
It was in this synagogue.
There, we were, again pushed on to the river.
There, the bridge was destroyed, but two bridges, really.
You have one a railway bridge, and one passenger foot bridge-- and were destroyed.
And they had a ferry boat there, which was spanned over and [INAUDIBLE] over the river.
And so was the-- were both sides.
They pushed the deportees, if I could call them.
So on the boat, my father, sister, late mother succeeded on the boat.
I had a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] from my late [INAUDIBLE] father bar mitzvah present which I didn't give over.
And I stretch out my hands with the watch dangling.
It was a pocket watch dangling.
And in the moment, the boat started moving.
It was a Romanian soldier pushed out the hand to get the watch.
So I grabbed his hand to pull me up on the boat.
So I was the last on the boat.
It took about 20 minute, half an hour to cross the river.

On the river, in addition to the deportee, there are carriages with Ukrainian driver, which he brought to the front to put in front kerosene, and benzene, and whatever they had there on the front for the army.
I could speak Ukrainian.
And I heard some discussion, oh, they get to their death.
They don't know what is to expect.
When I heard this, of course, I tried to reach my parents, my sister.
I couldn't, but, uh, in the meantime, was one incident which I will never forget.
Not far from me, a young woman had a child and the babies.
And one was a-- the soldier-- when a soldier grabbed the child and threw it in the-- the moms are like mad, jumped after, and he shot, they shot.
I don't know if she survived or if she was wounded.
I couldn't see.
It was darkening when we reach the Ukrainian side.
That was the town Mogilev-Podol'skii.
[NON-ENGLISH], which had originally Jewish native-- Jewish population where only a few left.
There were Jews from Bessarabia, which were taking over.
And they played with them.
They sent him over and back and forward to German.
They were there.
And Jews from the south and Bukovina, Romanian citizen, which were not under the Russian but they are also deported before us.
And they were there having money, Romanian money, speaking the lingo.
They are-- and in German they give money and arrange with the authority both German and the Romanian to let them for the time being.
They permitted 500, but they close their eyes for the time being.
They were in schools.
And some, which could pay THEM private houses, Jewish houses, permitt-- they were not permitting during the night to go out.
The were not permitted to go in the market only one hours in the day or two hours in the day.
But if you could speak to the local population and had the money, you could buy.
Now we landed the fir-- the deportees from Cernauti.
I had to bring then here more growth they had, eh, intended become.
They killed people, and tortured, and send them on and [INAUDIBLE] for [NON-ENGLISH] and also to Bajora, which was, uh, where they killed the Jews straightaway.
I was-- one was the first to come down.
Being the last to be on the [INAUDIBLE].
I tried to escape.

Not far away in the [INAUDIBLE], so many pushing, and shouting, and-- we are officer, militia, uh, Ukraine militia, Gestapo officer and some in uniform, and some in, uh, [? nafti. ?] Of course, SS officer, SS soldiers, Romanian mostly soldiers.
I try to escape.
And I jumped in the trenches, which were near they were the so-called [NON-ENGLISH, Stalin line.
I jumped into [INAUDIBLE], a rooksack.
It was back on the-- I pushed it down in the trenches and I returned.
I was shouting in German, so they took me as one of the Gestapo officers, speaking [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was searching, looking out for my family.
First, I found my little sister.
I took her.
She was about nine at that time-- uh, 10.
I took her out and brought her to my luggage there.
Sit here, wait till I come.
And she was sitting alone in the night in a strange country, frightened.
I found my late father.
I took him out and I sent him to go along the trenches to-- there if we find my sister.
I couldn't find my late mother.
I was searching and going towards the river.
I found her.
She had galoshes on and stuck in the mud.
With the heavy rooksack on the back, she stuck in the mud and couldn't go out.
I took her out and I brought her, so I had my family together.
Now where to go-- we started moving away from the transport along the trenches.
It was dark night.
Suddenly we heard heavy going, heavy boots.
Three Romanian soldiers with a woman, Ukrainian woman, she spoke Ukrainian-- stop.
We paid them.
We had still money left.
We paid them, and they let us go.
They took two of our rooksacks away and let us go.
We moved along the trenches.
We didn't know where we are.
We see-- during the day, we see where we are.
We could all speak Ukrainian, which was the local language.
I remember we had again some with the running, heavy boots are running after us.
They said, the Romanian soldier came back without the girl.
And, uh, they very afraid, we assume they're very afraid that we will not be caught by [INAUDIBLE] patrol.
So they say, come forward, forward.
And they brought us-- we saw from far there was a fire in a yard.
We didn't know it was a school.
And in the middle of the yard was a fire and bad Jews.
Afterwards, we found out it was a rebel from [NON-ENGLISH].
They shot the rebel, was there with his Hasidism.
We were sitting there.
We were deported from Suceava.
He had some elder left, Jewish, we discuss with the soldier which were guarding them.
Any case, they opened the gate and let us in.
So there is where other Jewish women is there.
They gave us hot tea to drink.
We were frozen, cold.
It was November in the Ukraine.
There was already snow on the Earth.
We were the first night, detention, so cold.
During the night, they searched for forced labor.
I was one of the lucky one to be put on the list.
But I-- in the morning they called.
I gave a false name.
And in the morning, they called out, so I did not react to this.
But right out, my father hid in the other school.
A cousin, he was from south Bukovina, and he went to ask how-- what-- how was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said, look, you are here in the school where the South Bukovina's are.
And they will catch you as you escape from the transport, and it's not good.
Look, here have some money.
You have some money.
Get the-- with somewhere in the town near the schools there.
There was a Russian with Jews, which gave us a room with an Auschwitz-Cernauti family, Hagar.
They're a religious family-- they were father, grandfather, a grandmother, mother and three children, two boys and a girl.
And they were there in hiding.
They had-- the door was camouflaged.
You couldn't see that this a door.
So when they came searching for vodka we had to keep quiet till they left.
During the day in the two hours or three hours where we were permitted to go, we went out to buying bread.
We had to pay for the bread, which was about 10 ruble, normally 200 ruble.
But we had bread.
And we saw the money go in.
So they had announcement.
They sent groups to get us in the country.
There, you will be a [INAUDIBLE].
You will work the colony.
So we went to the Zumwalt plants, where they directed the [INAUDIBLE].

On the first of December in the morning-- I remember the day because it was my birthday-- we went-- one carriage, we paid for a carriage all the Jews into there, hundred Jews-- men, women, and children, all the young, and to went under escort.
Who paid for the escort?
My late father arranged all this for the carriage, and the escort.
They went-- it was a very difficult way.
It was ice, and you had to walk and walk and some were falling.
They helped us with the arrival-- pushing us, putting us.
Any case, I remember it was a young man who wanted to run, let them shoot me.
I, recall, manhandled him.
I stopped him, and I kept him with me.
Now he is-- alive, still alive in Canada I heard.
And we went up in night, the first night, we stopped in [?
Servay-- ?] near a village.
We went up [INAUDIBLE] village.
It was the stable of a local [NON-ENGLISH]-- empty stable, of course, of a local [NON-ENGLISH].
They say, call it a farm.
It was stinking there from the-- you can imagine.
My late father couldn't breathe.
They say, we have to come out from there.
And I need a bit of air.
So I found-- to found the hole.
The guard which was there told us not to go out to be shoot.
But our luck, nobody was there.
We went to the village.
We went out.
We wanted to buy something in the village.
We neared the village, the only house which was-- we saw light and just the militia, our luck.
We entered, but we paid them and they gave us a bread or something that we returned.
We asked, where we are.
We didn't know.
Next day, we continued.
The guards came back-- fowards.
We went on towards in the direction of Shargorod.
But there was a colony called Lozova.
It was about eight kilometer in village-- 8 kilometer-- at each village, which had three [NON-ENGLISH], three farms.
And we stopped there outside.
My father went with one of the guards, the Romanian soldiers in the-- down in the village to spoke with the mayor, arrange for us to come in the village.
They told us, if they will accept you, you can stay there under escort, of course.
We came in the village, what we didn't know is we have prisoner of war camp there, which was not very much in our favor.
Because there again, soldiers there, and the Russian Ukrainian prisoner of war, Russian prisoner of war.
We came, in the village were two Jewish family which survived.
They-- you couldn't distinguish where working.
In the [NON-ENGLISH], because you were born there, you couldn't distinguish one from the local population.
And they survived.
And the-- the mayor told  us there are some Jews here and to speak with them, and he will help us.
He was a member of the Communist Party.
And the local [NON-ENGLISH], so they knew what he was, a foreman from the red fleet, which-- in Leningrad, [INAUDIBLE] we had, perhaps.
There, he was hero of the village.
And he continued being their many-- mayor.
My father spoke with him, arrange with him, we came in the village.
First night, we slept with these two-- all the hundred-- these two Jewish families.
They gave us hot tea and water and we settled.
Then we started speaking and they helped our-- we paid with clothes and what we have.
The local populations, they let us in the room with them in their-- [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH], a room in their house, which I had to pay.
We started living there.
We were not permitted [INAUDIBLE] to leave the village.
We had three or four soldiers which were permanently there.
And the soldiers which looted us were mostly from the prisoner of war camp.
I was called to the prisoner of war camp 1, because I had the list of the hundred which came there.
And the Praetor, that was remain in highest authority in Shargorod, wanted to see me.
So I came there with Mr.
Hagar.
I learned his son who was sent there.
And he asked me--he wanted say to make the list of the Jews age, everything.
I was there as student, written, or something.
We returned home.
It was night, a bitter night, and cold and the snow is this-- that became as a [INAUDIBLE] from the [NON-ENGLISH], but it left.
Nobody was on the way.
Only we had animals [INAUDIBLE] and this, but nobody.
We came back and we started preparing for the worse.
In the-- in Shargorod, there were Jews from south and Bukovina had organized a Judenrat.
It was-- the head was a Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH], a local lawyer, which arranged with the Romanian authority, except with-- the Praetor was a beast, inhuman [INAUDIBLE].
I remember, his name, Denvulik, Dan.
His hobby was beating Jews.
He went just beating.
And they had the Germans and Germany-- small, got his own [INAUDIBLE], so [INAUDIBLE].
But they were not active.
They were most in the region against from a Communist soul-searching.
Because with the Jews, you could speak.
They were the only from Bukovina that could speak German.
And in my times, it did not kill us so-- they were probably paid.
I couldn't tell you.
In any case, everything went through the Judenrat.
The winter was a bad winter, minus 40 degrees.
Many-- most of us, including our family, had typhoid fever, and lice, and hunger, and cold.
Many died, more than half in Shargorod In our case, less when you could-- could wash ourselves.
There are not many and-- uh, who worked.
You see, the German asked from the local population, uh, food, and also gloves, woven gloves, different woolen things, which look like a-- uh, population didn't know how to make.
Our people knew, they-- so they started working.
And they give them food to us to eat.
And says, I worked in the stables.
I worked as laborer.
And I got friendly with the local population, visit priest, which was in the village.
It was a old priest who couldn't see.
And I, from my years in the church school, could read the Slavonic language.
So I was reading in the church service, the [NON-ENGLISH], in his place.
He gave me to eat bread and this.
He lived in the house of the chief of Ukrainian militia.
So I was persona grata there.
I got to eat, and my father knew well, eh, the tobacco.
So I worked for the tobacco plant which he had drying in this-- in the [NON-ENGLISH].
